focus.title: Excessive use of vocal fillers
focus.explanation: The constant repetition of the filler sound 'em' throughout your responses creates a disjointed rhythm that significantly hinders a smooth and natural flow of ideas.

tokens.tense_term: Infinitive passive with modal verb: should
Infinitive passive with modal verb: can

17761786988996440.question: Describe a time when you had to solve a complex problem. What approach did you take and what was the outcome?

fc.comment: The speaker starts with "To resolve" but then changes the sentence structure, making it a false start.
The speaker repeats the word "for".
The speaker repeats the word "for".
The word "try" is redundant when followed immediately by "keep trying".
"No only" appears to be a false start or a slip of the tongue.
The conjunction "and" is redundant when "including" is used.
The speaker repeats "he when when he".
The phrase "what kind of" is a filler/redundant start that doesn't fit the sentence structure.
The speaker repeats "it it".
The word "some" is redundant before "someone".
The word "reason" is redundant because the sentence already began with "The reason".
Starting a sentence with "Although" makes the use of "but" later in the same sentence redundant.
The speaker repeats the article "the".
The speaker repeats "the in the".
The speaker repeats the pronoun "it".
The speaker repeats "some".
The speaker repeats "to".
The speaker stutters/repeats "the bet bet as".
The conjunction "and" is used to list items more naturally.
The word "outside" is repeated.
"End" is a false start before the speaker says "understand."
The phrase "more to resolve, think of" is wordy and redundant; removing it clarifies the sentence.
The speaker repeats "it's".

gr.comment: The speaker is talking about a past event ("Recently"), so the past tense "resolved" is used.
Adding ", which was" creates a relative clause that correctly connects the problem to its description.
The speaker is referring to a past action, so "used" is more appropriate.
In this context, the definite article "the" is unnecessary before the proper name of the software.
The context is a past experience, so the past tense "worked" is used.
The adverb "quickly" should be used to modify the verb "worked".
The past tense "was" is used to maintain consistency with the rest of the narrative.
The past tense "was" is used as the speaker is describing a past situation.
"the" is added to make "the model" specific.
The definite article "the" is removed because "probability" is used as an abstract general concept.
The plural "mistakes" is needed for a general statement.
"It needed" is a more standard and grammatically correct way to express requirement in this context than "there's need".
The speaker is referring to a past approach, so "was" is used.
The infinitive "to" is added after "just" to correctly link it to the verb phrase.
"Verifying" (gerund) is used to maintain parallelism with "trying".
The plural "results" is more appropriate as multiple attempts were likely verified.
The gerund "verifying" acts as the subject of the clause, replacing the awkward "the verified".
The article "the" is removed to streamline the gerund phrase "verifying the results".
The plural "results" is used for consistency.
The third-person singular "keeps" is required for agreement with the subject "a tool".
The indefinite article "a" is used for a non-specific question.
"Human experience" is a standard noun phrase; the possessive "human's" is less common here.
The article "the" is unnecessary before "time" in the phrase "from all time".
"From" is added to create a parallel structure with "from everywhere".
The definite article "the" is added for grammatical correctness ("the experience of...").
"Knowledge" is an uncountable noun and does not take a plural form.
The plural "humans" is used to refer to people in general.
The present perfect "have accumulated" is used to describe an action that started in the past and continues to the present.
The article "the" is removed because "history" is used in a general sense.
Artificial intelligence should be referred to with the neutral pronoun "it" rather than "he". Instances of repetition within this tag are also removed.
The auxiliary verb "does" is needed for the negative statement "does not understand".
Subject-verb agreement: "it hears".
The plural "conversations" is used for a general statement.
The definite article is removed for a more general reference to adults.
The article "the" is removed before the acronym "AI".
The position of "be" is moved to after the adverb "always" for better flow.
The verb "be" is placed after "always" ("always be correct").
The verb "be" is added to complete the phrase "can also be like".
The indefinite article "a" is needed before "human".
The definite article "the" is added before "root".
"The china" is grammatically incorrect; it should just be "Chinese art" (corrected in r_54).
The pronoun "it" is redundant after the subject "The reason I like it".
The past tense "liked" is used because the speaker is talking about their childhood.
The past tense "loved" is used for consistency with the past narrative.
The plural "gardens" is used for a general reference to traditional spaces.
The definite article "the" is added for grammatical completeness.
The present tense "get" is used for consistency with "practice".
The article "the" is removed before the gerund "practicing".
The singular "thing" is used in the phrase "sort of thing".
The verb "like" is added to complete the modal phrase "would always like to".
The indefinite article "a" is added before "new world".
The base form "use" is used for parallelism with "learn" and "experience".
The indefinite article "a" is added before "new language".
The definite article "the" is unnecessary before "people" in this general context.
"Get frustrated" is a more natural and direct expression than "get into frustration."
The article "the" is removed before "life" when used in a general sense.
The possessive pronoun "your" is added to clarify whose lifestyle is being referred to.
The preposition "in" is added to complete the phrase "living in a different way."
"Revolution for the world" is more idiomatic than "revolution of the world" in this context.
The order and preposition are changed to "for almost everything" for better flow.
The plural "humans" is more appropriate when referring to the entire human race.
The plural "problems" is used for a general statement.
The gerund "understanding" is used to match the parallel structure of "resolving."
The plural pronoun "them" is used to refer back to "problems."
The article "the" is removed before "technical difficulties" for a more general reference.
The plural "difficulties" is used to reflect the variety of possible technical issues.
"Something" is added to complete the relative clause "that humans have never experienced."

lr.comment: The field of study is called "statistics" (plural form).
"Guesses" better fits the context of an AI predicting an answer.
"All over the world" is the correct idiomatic phrase.
"The reason" is a more precise and logical way to begin the sentence than "the way".
In English, we "practice" an art or a skill rather than "exercise" it.
The word "since" is used incorrectly here and is removed.
"Practicing" is the correct term for engaging in an art form.
"A sense of achievement" is a common and correct idiomatic expression.
"Zen" is the correct term for the peaceful, meditative state the speaker is describing.
"Meet people" is the standard collocation for making new acquaintances.
The idiom is "expand my horizons," not "horizontal."
"Drastically" is the correct word to describe large-scale, impactful change; "traumastically" is likely a confusion with "traumatically" or "drastically."
"Just" is used instead of "to" to provide better emphasis and logical connection.

idiomatic_list.get out of my comfort zone: to push yourself to do things that are new, challenging, or uncomfortable in order to grow or improve
